revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:completion tier 2]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:qq]][[en:Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |名称 = Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine |image = [[File:Sumiyoshi Otomo-jinja, haiden.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}{{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}{{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} Ryōanji Sumiyoshi-chō 1 |ISO = JP-26 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 01|緯度秒 = 54 |経度度 = 135|経度分 = 43|経度秒 = 2 |ラベル位置 = right |地図 = Japan Kyoto city |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}}
{{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}}
{{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} |type = Former {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} with cypress bark roof, gabled |別名 = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = October 16 |神事 = }} '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine''' (住吉大伴神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}} of {{ill|Kyoto City|ja|京都市}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, near Ryōanji Temple. It holds the status of a former village shrine under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Sumiyoshi Mountain|ja|住吉山}}, south of the {{ill|Kyoto City Road 183|ja|京都市道183号}} (Kinukake-no-Michi). It is 150 meters southwest of {{ill|Ninna-ji|ja|仁和寺}} and 150 meters northeast of {{ill|Ryōan-ji|ja|龍安寺}}. The shrine serves as the {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}} for the local area, including Ryōanji and Taniguchi in {{ill|Ukyō Ward|ja|右京区}}.Heibonsha (1997), p.404 == Deities Enshrined == * The ancestral deities of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} - {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} (あめのおしひのみこと) - {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと) * The {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神}} - Uwatsutsu-no-mikoto (上筒男命) - Nakatsutsu-no-mikoto (中筒男命) - Sokotsutsu-no-mikoto (底筒男命) Source: {{ill|Kyoto Ancient Shrines and Temples Dictionary|ja|京都古社寺辞典}} == History == ; Banshi Shrine The year of foundation is unknown, but it is believed to have been established as the ancestral shrine of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} when they moved from Yamato Province to the Heian capital during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.32 An entry in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}} for the 11th year of Tenchō (834) mentions the founding of Banshi Shrine, which may refer to the present Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine or the Banshi Shrine within the precincts of {{ill|Kitano Tenmangū|ja|北野天満宮}}.Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage (1994), p.44 In the 14th year of Kōnin (823), when Emperor Junna ascended the throne, the Ōtomo clan changed their name to the Banshi clan in honor of the emperor’s personal name. The shrine was then renamed '''Banshi Shrine''' (伴氏神社). The name appears in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} under Kamikaya-go in Kadono District, Yamashiro Province. After the Ōtomo clan declined following the {{ill|Ōtenmon Incident|ja|応天門の変}} in Jōgan 6 (866), the shrine's ceremonies fell into disuse.Makino et al. (2007), p.40 ; Sumiyoshi Shrine After the decline of the Ōtomo clan, the area came under the control of the Tokudaiji family, who worshipped the Sumiyoshi deities and renamed the shrine '''Sumiyoshi Shrine'''. ; Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine In the Shōwa era, there was a renewed call to honor the ancestral deities of the Ōtomo clan. In 1942, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天忍日命|1=Ama-no-Oshihi}} and {{ill|Michinoomi|ja|道臣命}} were enshrined alongside the Sumiyoshi deities, and the shrine was renamed '''Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine'''.Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department (2010), p.188 == Grounds == ; Shrine Buildings The current main hall, built in the early Shōwa period, is a reduced-scale {{ill|Sumiyoshi-zukuri|ja|住吉造}} structure, unique in Japan.History plaque at Sumiyoshi Ōtomo Shrine. In 1978, its cypress bark roof was replaced with copper sheeting. Sumiyoshi-zukuri architecture is otherwise seen only at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in Osaka and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|ja|住吉神社 (福岡市)}} in Fukuoka. ; Other Shrines Originally, the grounds included Tenzen Gongen Shrine, Sai-no-Miya Shrine, and Komatsuo Myōjin Shrine. Today, only Komatsuo Shrine remains, with the others merged into it. ; Monument The precincts house a monument engraved with the poem by {{ill|Ōtomo no Yakamochi|ja|大伴家持}}: "海ゆかばみつく屍 山ゆかば 草むす屍 大君のへにこそ死なめ かへりみはせし," featured in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} Volume 18. This poem inspired the Japanese national song {{ill|Umi Yukaba|ja|海行かば}}. File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI1.JPG|From Kinukake-no-Michi File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI2.JPG|Shrine office File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI3.JPG|Komatsuo Shrine File:Sumiyoshi Otomo Shrine140606NI5.JPG|Torii and precincts == Access == * Nearest station: {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|ja|京福電気鉄道}}{{ill|Kitano Line|ja|京福電気鉄道北野線}} at either {{ill|Myōshin-ji Station|ja|妙心寺駅}} or {{ill|Ryōan-ji Station|ja|龍安寺駅}}, 400m north * Nearest bus stop: Kyoto City Bus "Tōnoshitachō" or "Ryōanji-mae," 150m away == References == * Kyoto City Bureau of Cultural Heritage, ''Kyoto's Ancient Shrines and Temples - Kyoto's Engishiki Shrines and Ancient Temples'' (Kyoto City Cultural Heritage Books), 1994. * Makino Osamu, supervised by Yamori Tetsuo, ''505 Kyoto Temples and Shrines to Visit, Vol. 2 - Western, Northern, Southern, and Outer Kyoto'' (PHP Shinsho), PHP Institute, 2007. * Heibonsha, ''Kyoto-Yamashiro Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia'', Heibonsha, 1997. * Nishida Naojirō, ''A Short History of Rakusai Hanazono'', Sekizenkan, 1945. * Yoshikawa Kōbunkan Editorial Department, ''Kyoto Ancient Shrine and Temple Encyclopedia'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2010. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Tomobayashi Shrine|ja|伴林氏神社}} - A shrine in Fujidera City, Osaka, also enshrining Michinoomi and related to the Ōtomo clan. {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}}
